0-2.question: What is the difference between .NET Framework and modern .NET (Core / .NET 5+)?
0-2.answer: .NET Framework is Windows-only, ships with the OS, and supports WinForms, WPF, and ASP.NET Web Forms. Modern .NET (.NET Core, then .NET 5/6/7/8+) is cross-platform (Windows, Linux, macOS), open-source, high-performance, and ships independently. For new projects, always use modern .NET. ASP.NET Core replaced ASP.NET MVC/Web API and runs on all platforms.
0-2.simpleExample: .NET Framework is like an old Windows-only car — powerful but can't leave Windows territory. Modern .NET is like a Tesla — cross-platform, faster, regularly updated, and open-source. For any new project today, use modern .NET. Think .NET 8 or .NET 9 as your starting point.

0-2.question: What is async/await in C# and how does it work?
0-2.answer: async/await is C#'s syntax for writing asynchronous code that reads like synchronous code. When a method is marked async, it can use await to pause execution at an I/O-bound operation (HTTP call, DB query, file read) without blocking the thread. The thread is freed to do other work while waiting, then resumes when the awaited operation completes. This is critical for building scalable web APIs.
0-2.simpleExample: Without async/await: a waiter takes your order, goes to the kitchen, and STANDS THERE staring at the cook until the food is ready — blocking every other customer. With async/await: the waiter takes your order, goes to the kitchen, then serves OTHER tables while your food cooks. When it's ready, they come back and serve you. Way more efficient.

0-2.question: What is LINQ in C# and how is it used?
0-2.answer: LINQ (Language Integrated Query) is a set of extension methods on IEnumerable<T> and IQueryable<T> that lets you query, filter, transform, and aggregate data using a SQL-like syntax directly in C#. It works on any collection (arrays, lists, DB results via Entity Framework). Key methods: Where, Select, OrderBy, GroupBy, First/FirstOrDefault, Any, All, Count, Sum, Aggregate, ToList, ToDictionary.
0-2.simpleExample: Without LINQ, filtering a list means writing a foreach loop with if statements every single time. LINQ gives you reusable 'SQL for collections' — filter with Where, transform with Select, sort with OrderBy, take the first match with First. Write less code, read it like English: 'get all active employees, sort by salary, take the top 5.'

0-2.question: What is Entity Framework Core and how do you perform basic CRUD operations?
0-2.answer: Entity Framework Core (EF Core) is .NET's official ORM (Object-Relational Mapper). It maps C# classes (entities) to database tables, lets you write C# LINQ queries instead of SQL, and handles migrations (schema changes). The DbContext is the main class you work with — it represents the database session. EF Core supports SQL Server, PostgreSQL, SQLite, MySQL, and others.
0-2.simpleExample: Writing raw SQL in your C# code is like speaking a foreign language every time you need data. EF Core is your translator — you write C# and it generates the SQL for you. You define 'Product' as a class, EF creates the 'Products' table. You write LINQ, EF writes the SQL query. Migrations track your schema changes like Git tracks code changes.

0-2.question: What are Value Types vs Reference Types in C#?
0-2.answer: Value types (struct, int, double, bool, DateTime, enum) store their data directly on the stack. Assigning a value type creates a COPY. Reference types (class, string, arrays, interfaces) store a reference (pointer) on the stack to the actual data on the heap. Assigning a reference type copies the reference — both variables point to the same object. This distinction matters for performance and for understanding mutation behavior.
0-2.simpleExample: Value types are like writing a phone number on paper — if you photocopy the paper, you get a completely independent copy. Reference types are like a shared Google Doc — you can give multiple people the link, but everyone's editing the SAME document. Change it through one link and everyone sees it. Strings are special: they look like shared docs but are actually immutable — every edit creates a new document.

2-4.question: What is Dependency Injection in ASP.NET Core and what are the three service lifetimes?
2-4.answer: ASP.NET Core has a built-in DI container. Services are registered in Program.cs and injected via constructor (or primary constructor in C# 12+). Three lifetimes: Singleton (one instance for the entire app lifetime — use for stateless services, caches), Scoped (one per HTTP request — use for DbContext, per-request state), Transient (new instance every time it's requested — use for lightweight, stateless services).
2-4.simpleExample: Singleton: one coffee machine for the whole office — everyone uses the same one, forever. Scoped: a fresh notepad per meeting (HTTP request) — torn up when the meeting ends. Transient: a disposable pen — get a new one every time you ask, throw it away when done. Pick the right lifetime: DbContext = Scoped, global config service = Singleton, validators = Transient.

2-4.question: What is Middleware in ASP.NET Core and how does the request pipeline work?
2-4.answer: Middleware is software assembled into a pipeline to handle HTTP requests and responses. Each middleware component can perform operations before and after the next component in the pipeline. Order matters — middleware runs in the order it's added. The pipeline is the backbone of ASP.NET Core: authentication, routing, rate limiting, exception handling, CORS, compression, and logging are all middleware.
2-4.simpleExample: The request pipeline is like airport security lines. Your HTTP request (passenger) passes through checkpoints in ORDER: HTTPS check, static files, routing, authentication, authorization. Each checkpoint can let you through, turn you back, or add something to your bag (response headers). If you put authorization BEFORE routing, it doesn't know which endpoint you're going to. Order is everything.

2-4.question: What are the SOLID principles and how do they apply in .NET?
2-4.answer: SOLID is an acronym for five design principles: Single Responsibility (a class should have one reason to change), Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification — use interfaces/inheritance), Liskov Substitution (subtypes must be substitutable for base types), Interface Segregation (prefer small focused interfaces over large ones), Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions, not concrete classes — enables DI and testing).
2-4.simpleExample: SOLID is like a well-run kitchen. SRP: each chef has ONE job (pastry, grill, prep). OCP: add new dishes without rewriting existing recipes. LSP: any chef brand of butter can substitute another in any recipe. ISP: waiters only know about taking orders, not cooking. DIP: recipes call for 'butter', not a specific brand — swap brands without changing the recipe.

2-4.question: What are common Design Patterns used in .NET and C#?
2-4.answer: Key patterns: Repository (abstracts data access behind an interface), Unit of Work (groups multiple DB operations into one transaction), Factory (creates objects without exposing creation logic), Strategy (swap algorithms at runtime via interfaces), Observer (event-driven updates — C# events/delegates), Decorator (add behavior without modifying the class), Options Pattern (.NET's way of loading strongly-typed config from appsettings.json).
2-4.simpleExample: Repository: put a 'librarian' between your code and the database — your code asks the librarian for books, doesn't shuffle shelves itself. Strategy: a GPS app has multiple 'route strategies' (fastest, shortest, no tolls) — you switch between them without rewriting the app. Options pattern: instead of magic strings like Configuration["Smtp:Host"], you get a typed class with intellisense and compile-time safety.

2-4.question: What is the difference between IEnumerable, IQueryable, and when should you use each?
2-4.answer: IEnumerable<T> loads data into memory, then filters/transforms in C# (client-side). IQueryable<T> builds an expression tree that is translated into SQL (or another query language) and executed on the database server, only bringing back the needed data. Always use IQueryable for EF Core queries so that WHERE clauses, ordering, and pagination happen in the database, not in memory.
2-4.simpleExample: IEnumerable is like moving your entire filing cabinet to your desk to find one document — you search through everything in your hands. IQueryable is like telling the filing room clerk what you need — they find it at the source and bring only what you asked for. For database queries, ALWAYS use IQueryable and only call ToList()/ToListAsync() at the very end to execute it.
